gold_standard_answer: HER2 exon 20 insertion in NSCLC (distinct from HER2 amplification in breast cancer): (1) First-line: no approved HER2-targeted therapy for first-line NSCLC; standard chemo-IO (platinum/pemetrexed/pembrolizumab) is recommended; (2) Second-line and beyond: trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) is approved for HER2-mutant NSCLC based on DESTINY-Lung02 (ORR ~49%); (3) HER2 mutations are found in ~2–4% of NSCLC, most commonly exon 20 insertions; (4) Standard EGFR TKIs are ineffective against HER2 mutations; (5) Ongoing clinical trials are exploring first-line T-DXd and combinations; (6) Confirm that this is a mutation (activating insertion) rather than amplification, as treatment differs.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 HER2-1

vignette: A 72-year-old female with stage IV NSCLC squamous cell carcinoma progresses after first-line pembrolizumab + carboplatin + paclitaxel (KEYNOTE-407). She completed 4 cycles of triplet and 6 months of pembrolizumab maintenance. ECOG PS 2.
question: What are appropriate second-line options for this patient with declining PS?
gold_standard_answer: For squamous NSCLC progressing after KEYNOTE-407 with PS 2: (1) Docetaxel at reduced dose (60 mg/m² instead of 75) is an option; (2) Ramucirumab addition should be avoided given PS 2 and added toxicity; (3) Weekly paclitaxel or gemcitabine may be better tolerated single-agent options; (4) Afatinib is approved for squamous NSCLC (LUX-Lung 8) and is an oral option; (5) Best supportive care should be an explicit option in the discussion; (6) Check for any missed molecular targets (FGFR, PIK3CA in squamous); (7) Clinical trials with appropriate PS eligibility; (8) Early palliative care integration.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-20

vignette: A 55-year-old male with stage IV NSCLC achieved complete response on pembrolizumab monotherapy and stopped treatment at 2 years. Now, 14 months after stopping, surveillance CT shows a new 1.8cm pulmonary nodule and mildly enlarged mediastinal lymph node.
question: How should recurrence after immunotherapy discontinuation be managed?
gold_standard_answer: Recurrence after IO discontinuation: (1) Tissue biopsy of the new nodule to confirm recurrence vs. new primary; (2) Full restaging: PET-CT, brain MRI; (3) Repeat molecular profiling on the new specimen—tumor biology may have changed; (4) Repeat PD-L1 testing; (5) If confirmed recurrence: pembrolizumab rechallenge is a reasonable first approach—KEYNOTE-024 and retrospective data show ~50–60% of patients respond to IO rechallenge after planned discontinuation; (6) If limited recurrence (oligorecurrence): consider local therapy (surgery or SBRT) + IO rechallenge; (7) If rechallenge fails: platinum-based chemotherapy ± IO; (8) Clinical trial enrollment if available.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-17; KEYNOTE-024 long-term data

vignette: A 64-year-old male with stage IVA NSCLC adenocarcinoma with oligometastatic disease: primary left lower lobe mass and 2 contralateral lung metastases only. No driver mutations. PD-L1 TPS 50%. ECOG PS 0.
question: What is the role of local consolidative therapy in this oligometastatic scenario?
gold_standard_answer: Oligometastatic NSCLC (limited metastatic burden) may benefit from aggressive multimodality: (1) Initiate systemic therapy first: pembrolizumab monotherapy (PD-L1 ≥50%) or chemo-IO; (2) After 3–4 cycles, if responding: local consolidative therapy (LCT) to all known disease sites using surgery and/or SBRT; (3) Multiple prospective trials (Gomez et al., Iyengar et al.) show PFS and OS benefit with LCT in oligometastatic NSCLC; (4) Continue systemic IO after local treatment; (5) Definition: generally ≤3–5 metastatic sites amenable to local therapy; (6) MDT discussion is essential to determine resectability and radiation planning; (7) This approach converts an incurable to a potentially long-term control scenario.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-17; Gomez et al. JCO 2019

vignette: A 60-year-old male former smoker (28 pack-years, quit 3 years ago) undergoes his third annual LDCT screening. The radiologist reports a new 5mm ground-glass nodule not present on prior scans.
question: How should a new GGN detected during serial screening be evaluated?
gold_standard_answer: New 5mm GGN on annual screening: Lung-RADS 2 (benign): (1) Continue annual LDCT screening; (2) No short-interval follow-up required for a pure GGN <6mm; (3) If the GGN was ≥6mm, Lung-RADS 3 would apply with 6-month follow-up; (4) Document for comparison on future scans; (5) Reinforce smoking cessation (quit only 3 years ago). Pure GGNs <6mm have very low malignancy risk and indolent natural history.
guideline_reference: NCCN Lung Cancer Screening v2.2025; Lung-RADS v1.1

vignette: A 63-year-old female with resected stage IIA NSCLC adenocarcinoma. Surgical pathology NGS reveals EGFR L858R mutation. She completed adjuvant cisplatin/vinorelbine × 4 cycles.
question: Is there a role for adjuvant targeted therapy in this patient?
gold_standard_answer: Adjuvant osimertinib is indicated for this patient: (1) ADAURA trial demonstrated significant DFS benefit for adjuvant osimertinib in stage IB–IIIA resected EGFR-mutant (exon 19del or L858R) NSCLC; (2) Updated OS data showed OS benefit for stage II–IIIA; (3) Duration: 3 years of adjuvant osimertinib after completion of adjuvant chemotherapy; (4) Adjuvant osimertinib is a Category 1 recommendation for stage IIA EGFR-mutant NSCLC; (5) Testing: EGFR testing should be performed on all resected non-squamous NSCLC for this reason; (6) Monitor for osimertinib toxicity during 3-year adjuvant course (ILD, QTc, skin effects); (7) This changes the paradigm of adjuvant therapy for early-stage NSCLC.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-13; ADAURA trial

vignette: A 57-year-old female with stage IVB NSCLC adenocarcinoma. Massive bilateral pleural effusions causing dyspnea. No driver mutations. PD-L1 TPS 80%. ECOG PS 3 (due to effusions). BMI 22.
question: How should this symptomatic patient with poor PS be initially managed?
gold_standard_answer: PS 3 due to reversible cause (effusions) requires a different approach than PS 3 from tumor burden: (1) Immediate bilateral thoracentesis for symptom relief and reassessment of PS after drainage; (2) Consider indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) or pleurodesis for recurrent effusions; (3) Pleural fluid for cytology and cell block (can be used for PD-L1 and molecular testing); (4) If PS improves to 0–2 after effusion management: pembrolizumab monotherapy (PD-L1 ≥50%) is appropriate; (5) If PS remains 3: best supportive care or cautious single-agent IO may be considered; (6) Chemo-IO is generally too toxic for PS 3 patients; (7) Re-evaluate PS 1–2 weeks after drainage before making systemic therapy decisions.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-17; Expert consensus

vignette: A 65-year-old male with NSCLC adenocarcinoma harboring a METex14 skipping mutation. He progressed after 10 months on capmatinib. ECOG PS 1.
question: What are the options after MET inhibitor progression?
gold_standard_answer: Post-MET inhibitor progression: (1) Consider tissue or liquid re-biopsy for resistance mechanism profiling (MET kinase domain mutations, MET amplification, bypass pathway activation such as EGFR, KRAS, or BRAF); (2) If secondary MET mutation identified: alternative MET inhibitor may have activity (switch capmatinib to tepotinib or vice versa may not be effective for on-target resistance); (3) Platinum-based chemotherapy ± immunotherapy is the standard next-line approach; (4) Check PD-L1 if not recently assessed; (5) Clinical trials exploring novel MET-targeting strategies; (6) Type Ib MET inhibitors and MET-directed ADCs are in development; (7) Antibody-drug conjugates (e.g., teliso-V) targeting MET are emerging options.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 MET-1; Emerging data

vignette: A 68-year-old male on crizotinib for ROS1-positive NSCLC reports visual disturbances—flashing lights and trails of light especially at night—after 3 weeks of treatment.
question: How should crizotinib-related visual disturbances be managed?
gold_standard_answer: Crizotinib visual effects are a well-known class effect (~60% of patients): (1) These are typically benign visual accommodation disturbances (photopsia, light trails, visual persistence); (2) Do NOT discontinue crizotinib—reassure the patient; (3) Advise caution with night driving; (4) Symptoms often diminish over time; (5) No ophthalmologic intervention needed for typical visual effects; (6) However, if visual acuity changes, visual field defects, or severe symptoms develop: ophthalmology referral; (7) Distinguish from optic neuritis or retinal pathology which would require treatment modification.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025; Crizotinib prescribing information

vignette: A 55-year-old female completed neoadjuvant nivolumab + chemotherapy followed by right upper lobectomy for stage IIIA NSCLC. Pathology shows major pathologic response (MPR, <10% viable tumor). She is scheduled for adjuvant nivolumab.
question: What is the significance of MPR and the adjuvant IO plan?
gold_standard_answer: Major pathologic response (MPR) after neoadjuvant chemo-IO has prognostic significance: (1) MPR (<10% viable tumor) and pathologic complete response (pCR) correlate with improved event-free survival and OS; (2) Adjuvant nivolumab for 1 year is recommended per CheckMate 77T protocol regardless of pathologic response; (3) The benefit of adjuvant IO after achieving MPR/pCR is debated—ongoing analysis may refine which patients benefit most; (4) Surveillance: CT every 6 months for 2 years, then annually; brain MRI per stage III protocol; (5) Monitor for delayed irAEs during adjuvant nivolumab; (6) MPR assessment: report as percentage of viable tumor in the resection specimen using standardized pathologic scoring.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-8; CheckMate 77T; KEYNOTE-671

vignette: A 60-year-old male diagnosed with stage IV NSCLC adenocarcinoma. No driver mutations detected. PD-L1 TPS is 0%. ECOG PS 1. He asks if immunotherapy can still help.
question: Is immunotherapy indicated for PD-L1 negative (<1%) NSCLC without driver mutations?
gold_standard_answer: Yes, IO remains part of first-line treatment even with PD-L1 <1%: (1) Chemo-IO combination: pembrolizumab + platinum + pemetrexed (KEYNOTE-189) showed OS benefit regardless of PD-L1 status, including PD-L1 <1%; (2) Nivolumab + ipilimumab + 2 cycles chemotherapy (CheckMate 9LA) is another option; (3) Pembrolizumab monotherapy is NOT recommended for PD-L1 <1%; (4) The magnitude of IO benefit is smaller in PD-L1 <1% compared to PD-L1 ≥50%, but still statistically significant; (5) Chemotherapy alone (without IO) is considered suboptimal for first-line treatment in fit patients; (6) TMB (tumor mutational burden) may be considered as an alternative biomarker for IO benefit prediction.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-17

vignette: A 60-year-old female with EGFR exon 19 del NSCLC on osimertinib for 24 months. New progression with bone and adrenal metastases. Liquid biopsy shows MET amplification. Tissue re-biopsy confirms retained adenocarcinoma histology.
question: What is the recommended approach for MET amplification-mediated osimertinib resistance?
gold_standard_answer: MET amplification is the most common identifiable resistance mechanism to osimertinib (~15–20%): (1) Osimertinib + MET inhibitor combination is the preferred approach: osimertinib + savolitinib (TATTON, SAVANNAH data), osimertinib + capmatinib, or osimertinib + tepotinib; (2) Several combinations are under investigation or available via expanded access; (3) Level of MET amplification matters: high copy number (≥10) or MET/CEP7 ratio ≥2 correlates with higher response to MET inhibitor addition; (4) If combination not accessible: amivantamab + lazertinib (MARIPOSA-2) or platinum chemotherapy are alternatives; (5) Continue osimertinib alongside MET inhibitor (do not discontinue EGFR TKI); (6) Clinical trial enrollment is encouraged.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-22; EGFR-4; Emerging data

vignette: A 71-year-old male on pembrolizumab develops new-onset type 1 diabetes with DKA (glucose 480 mg/dL, pH 7.15, ketones positive) after cycle 3. He had no prior history of diabetes.
question: How should immune-related diabetes and DKA be managed?
gold_standard_answer: IO-related type 1 diabetes with DKA is a rare (~1%) but potentially life-threatening irAE: (1) Immediate DKA management per standard protocol: IV insulin infusion, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, electrolyte correction (potassium monitoring); (2) ICU admission for DKA management; (3) Hold pembrolizumab during acute management; (4) Check C-peptide (will be low/undetectable) and anti-GAD antibodies; (5) Steroids are NOT effective for IO-related T1DM (unlike most irAEs)—beta-cell destruction is typically complete and irreversible; (6) Transition to basal-bolus insulin regimen once DKA resolves; (7) Endocrinology consultation for long-term diabetes management; (8) Pembrolizumab CAN be resumed once diabetes is well-controlled on insulin; (9) Patient education: this is permanent insulin-dependent diabetes; (10) Screen for concurrent endocrinopathies (thyroid, adrenal).
guideline_reference: NCCN Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities v1.2025

vignette: A 66-year-old female is 5 years out from lobectomy for stage IB NSCLC. She has had no recurrence. She is a former smoker (30 pack-years, quit at time of diagnosis).
question: What ongoing surveillance and cancer prevention measures are recommended beyond 5 years?
gold_standard_answer: Beyond 5 years post-resection: (1) Annual low-dose CT (LDCT) screening continues—she remains at risk for both recurrence and second primary lung cancer; (2) The risk of recurrence decreases significantly after 5 years but never reaches zero; (3) Annual physical examination with attention to symptoms; (4) Continued smoking cessation reinforcement; (5) Age-appropriate cancer screening (breast, colorectal, cervical) per NCCN guidelines; (6) Influenza and pneumococcal vaccination; (7) Cardiovascular risk assessment (smokers have elevated CV risk); (8) Bone density screening given post-menopausal status; (9) Pulmonary rehabilitation or exercise program for optimized lung function.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-15; NCCN Survivorship

vignette: A 55-year-old male undergoes LDCT screening showing a 12mm solid nodule with lobulated margins. He was also found to have an 18mm pulmonary embolism in the right lower lobe pulmonary artery on the same scan.
question: How should the concurrent findings of suspicious nodule and PE be managed?
gold_standard_answer: Concurrent PE and suspicious nodule both need management: (1) PE management: initiate anticoagulation (DOAC preferred, e.g., apixaban or rivarelbocixaban) immediately; (2) Cancer workup: the combination of PE and suspicious nodule raises concern for cancer-associated VTE (Trousseau syndrome); (3) PET-CT for the nodule (can be performed on anticoagulation); (4) Tissue diagnosis of the nodule: timing of biopsy must be coordinated with anticoagulation—CT-guided biopsy may need brief anticoagulation hold; EBUS is generally lower bleeding risk; (5) If malignancy confirmed: LMWH or DOAC for cancer-associated VTE (CARAVAGGIO, SELECT-D data support apixaban/edoxaban for cancer VTE); (6) Concurrent IVC filter is NOT routinely indicated unless anticoagulation is contraindicated.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025; NCCN Cancer-Associated VTE

vignette: A 67-year-old male with newly diagnosed stage IV NSCLC adenocarcinoma. NGS is pending. His oncologist needs to decide whether to start treatment or wait for molecular results. He is symptomatic with progressive dyspnea.
question: When is it appropriate to initiate treatment before molecular profiling results are available?
gold_standard_answer: Balancing urgency vs. molecular results: (1) For rapidly symptomatic patients who cannot safely wait for NGS (typical turnaround 2–3 weeks): initiate empiric platinum-based chemotherapy (carboplatin + pemetrexed) without immunotherapy; (2) Rationale for withholding IO: if a driver mutation is subsequently found, prior IO exposure complicates future TKI use (increased toxicity risk, particularly hepatotoxicity and pneumonitis); (3) Once molecular results return: (a) if actionable driver found → stop chemo, start targeted therapy; (b) if no driver → add IO to ongoing chemo; (4) Liquid biopsy for rapid turnaround (7–10 days) should be sent concurrently with tissue NGS; (5) Symptomatic management: thoracentesis, bronchoscopy for airway obstruction, radiation for hemoptysis or pain.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-5A; Expert consensus

vignette: A 52-year-old male with stage IV NSCLC. NGS reveals an uncommon EGFR mutation: G719A (exon 18). PD-L1 TPS 45%. ECOG PS 1.
question: How should an uncommon EGFR mutation be treated?
gold_standard_answer: Uncommon EGFR mutations require nuanced management: (1) G719X (exon 18) mutations are sensitive to certain TKIs but not all: afatinib has the strongest data for uncommon mutations (LUX-Lung 2/3/6 post-hoc analysis, ORR ~60%); (2) Osimertinib also has activity against G719X mutations (KCSG-LU15-09 trial and post-hoc FLAURA data); (3) First-generation TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib) have lower response rates for uncommon mutations; (4) Despite PD-L1 45%, targeted therapy is preferred; (5) Exon 20 insertions are the exception—resistant to most TKIs; (6) Compound uncommon mutations (e.g., G719X + S768I) may respond particularly well to afatinib or osimertinib.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 EGFR-2

vignette: A 63-year-old male on alectinib for ALK-positive NSCLC for 9 months develops progressive bilateral lower extremity edema, weight gain (5kg), and new-onset bradycardia (HR 45 bpm). Echocardiogram shows normal LVEF. He has no prior cardiac history.
question: What is causing these symptoms and how should they be managed?
gold_standard_answer: Alectinib-associated bradycardia and edema: (1) Bradycardia is a known class effect of ALK TKIs (~10% with alectinib): hold alectinib if HR <50 or symptomatic (dizziness, syncope); (2) Evaluate for concurrent medications causing bradycardia (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers); (3) Peripheral edema is very common with alectinib (~30%): manage with compression stockings, leg elevation, dietary sodium restriction; (4) Diuretics (low-dose furosemide) if edema is functionally limiting; (5) If bradycardia resolves after hold: restart alectinib at same or reduced dose (450mg BID → 300mg BID) with cardiac monitoring; (6) ECG monitoring: check for QTc prolongation; (7) If symptomatic bradycardia persists: cardiology consultation, consider pacemaker evaluation for long-term TKI use; (8) Do NOT switch ALK TKI solely for edema—all ALK TKIs can cause it.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 ALK-1; Alectinib prescribing information

vignette: A 58-year-old female was treated with definitive chemoradiation and durvalumab for stage IIIB NSCLC. At month 18 post-treatment, she develops new persistent cough and CT shows focal consolidation in the previously irradiated field. PET shows moderate FDG uptake.
question: How should post-radiation changes vs. local recurrence be differentiated?
gold_standard_answer: Distinguishing radiation fibrosis/pneumonitis from recurrence is challenging: (1) Post-radiation changes (fibrosis, organizing pneumonia) can be PET-avid for up to 12–24 months after RT; (2) Key features suggesting recurrence: growing mass-like consolidation, new nodularity outside the radiation field, increasing FDG avidity over serial scans; (3) Key features suggesting radiation change: conforming to the radiation field, stable or decreasing over time, ground-glass component; (4) Serial imaging at 6–8 week intervals to assess trajectory is often the first step; (5) If growing or highly suspicious: biopsy is essential (bronchoscopy preferred for central lesions, CT-guided for peripheral); (6) If biopsy shows recurrence: re-evaluate molecular profile and treat as recurrent disease; (7) If organizing pneumonia: trial of corticosteroids.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-15; Expert consensus

vignette: A 78-year-old female with stage IV NSCLC adenocarcinoma, EGFR exon 19 deletion. She has severe chronic kidney disease (CKD stage 4, eGFR 22). ECOG PS 2. She lives alone with limited social support.
question: What treatment approach is appropriate for this patient with significant renal impairment?
gold_standard_answer: Renal impairment affects treatment selection significantly: (1) Osimertinib is first-line for EGFR exon 19 del—it is hepatically metabolized and does NOT require dose adjustment for renal impairment; (2) Osimertinib is the ideal choice here as it avoids nephrotoxic chemotherapy; (3) Monitor for osimertinib-specific toxicities: QTc prolongation (baseline ECG), ILD, skin effects; (4) If osimertinib fails: platinum-based chemotherapy is problematic—cisplatin is contraindicated in CKD 4, carboplatin requires dose reduction with extreme caution; (5) Consider geriatric oncology assessment given age, PS, and social situation; (6) Home health or social work referral for medication management; (7) Goals of care discussion regarding treatment burden vs. benefit; (8) Ensure appropriate CKD management alongside cancer treatment (nephrology co-management).
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 EGFR-1; NCCN Older Adult Oncology

vignette: A 61-year-old female with stage IV NSCLC. Initially PD-L1 TPS 80%, received pembrolizumab monotherapy with excellent response for 18 months. Now progressing. Re-biopsy shows PD-L1 dropped to 5%. No driver mutations.
question: What does the PD-L1 change signify and what are the next steps?
gold_standard_answer: PD-L1 dynamic change and post-IO management: (1) PD-L1 expression can change over time and with treatment—this is well-documented; (2) Decreased PD-L1 may reflect immune editing or acquired IO resistance; (3) Treatment after pembrolizumab monotherapy progression: platinum-based chemotherapy is the standard (carboplatin + pemetrexed or carboplatin + paclitaxel); (4) Addition of IO to chemotherapy after prior IO monotherapy is controversial—limited data, but pembrolizumab + chemo may be considered; (5) Docetaxel ± ramucirumab as an alternative; (6) Re-check for any newly emerged molecular targets on the re-biopsy NGS; (7) Clinical trial enrollment; (8) PD-L1 re-testing at progression does not currently change standard treatment algorithms but is informative.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-20; Expert consensus

vignette: A 56-year-old male on sotorasib for KRAS G12C-mutant NSCLC develops Grade 3 transaminase elevation (ALT 8× ULN, AST 6× ULN) after 4 weeks of treatment. Bilirubin is normal. No symptoms.
question: How should sotorasib-related hepatotoxicity be managed?
gold_standard_answer: Sotorasib hepatotoxicity management: (1) Hold sotorasib immediately for Grade 3 hepatotoxicity (ALT/AST >5× ULN); (2) Rule out other causes: viral hepatitis serology, medications, liver metastases progression; (3) Monitor LFTs twice weekly; (4) Corticosteroids generally NOT required for TKI-related hepatotoxicity (unlike IO-related); (5) Once resolved to Grade ≤1: resume sotorasib at reduced dose (960mg → 480mg → 240mg dose reduction scheme); (6) If Grade 3 recurs at reduced dose: permanent discontinuation; (7) If Grade 4 (ALT/AST >20× ULN): permanent discontinuation; (8) Consider switching to adagrasib as alternative KRAS G12C inhibitor if sotorasib is not tolerable (different hepatotoxicity profile).
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 KRAS-1; Sotorasib prescribing information

vignette: A 69-year-old male with stage IIIA NSCLC underwent neoadjuvant chemo-IO then surgery. He is now 6 months post-surgery on adjuvant nivolumab. CT shows post-surgical changes but also a new 8mm nodule in the contralateral lung.
question: How should a new contralateral nodule during adjuvant immunotherapy be evaluated?
gold_standard_answer: New contralateral nodule during adjuvant IO: (1) Differential: early recurrence/metastasis, new primary lung cancer, or benign process; (2) Importantly, pseudoprogression (IO-related inflammatory changes) can manifest as new nodules—must be considered; (3) Short-interval CT at 6–8 weeks to assess growth trajectory; (4) If growing: PET-CT for metabolic characterization; (5) If PET-avid and growing: tissue biopsy for definitive diagnosis; (6) Continue adjuvant nivolumab unless biopsy-confirmed progression; (7) If recurrence confirmed: restage comprehensively (brain MRI, PET-CT) and transition to metastatic treatment paradigm; (8) If new primary: molecular profiling and staging as independent cancer; (9) If stable or resolving: may represent post-IO inflammatory response, continue surveillance.
guideline_reference: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-15; NSCL-8

vignette: A 53-year-old female, never-smoker, of Chinese descent, presents with persistent cough and mild hemoptysis. CT shows a 3cm left upper lobe mass with ground-glass halo, pleural tag, and air bronchogram. No mediastinal lymphadenopathy. She has a family history of lung cancer in her mother and maternal aunt (both never-smokers).
question: What is the clinical significance of the epidemiologic and radiologic features
gold_standard_answer: and what workup is recommended?
guideline_reference: This case has multiple clinically significant features: (1) Epidemiology: East Asian never-smoker female with family history—highest risk demographic for EGFR-mutant lung adenocarcinoma; (2) Radiologic features: mixed GGN with solid component, pleural tag, and air bronchogram are suggestive of adenocarcinoma (lepidic/acinar pattern); (3) Workup: (a) PET-CT for staging; (b) Tissue diagnosis: CT-guided biopsy or bronchoscopy depending on accessibility; (c) Ensure adequate tissue for molecular testing—NGS with reflexive EGFR, ALK, ROS1; (d) Brain MRI for complete staging; (4) Family clustering of lung cancer in never-smokers suggests possible germline susceptibility—refer to genetic counseling; (5) If confirmed early-stage: surgical resection with consideration of segmentectomy given peripheral GGN-predominant morphology; (6) Strong pre-test probability for actionable driver mutation—molecular results are critical for treatment planning regardless of stage.
column8: NCCN NSCLC v3.2025 NSCL-1; NSCL-5A; NCCN Genetic/Familial Assessment
